findings.title: forge_update_run allows arbitrary worktreePath injection
findings.description: The forge_update_run tool accepts a free-form `worktreePath` string with zero validation. An attacker (or manipulated model) can call forge_update_run with worktreePath set to any absolute path on the filesystem (e.g. 'C:\Users\cuj\sensitive-project'). When forge_create_run later resolves apply-stage worktrees (server.js lines 1280-1297), it checks `existsSync(impl.worktreePath)` and trusts whatever path is stored. The apply pipeline then operates inside that arbitrary directory — reading, writing, and potentially committing files in a project the run was never associated with. The runId schema blocks traversal in run IDs but worktreePath has no containment check (e.g. must be under .worktrees/).
findings.exploitability: Medium — requires a model prompt injection or a compromised agent to call forge_update_run with a crafted worktreePath. The path must exist on disk. Once set, the next /forge:apply run will operate in the attacker-chosen directory.

findings.title: forge_update_run allows status bypass of gate enforcement
findings.description: forge_update_run (server.js line 1449) allows setting any run's status to 'completed' directly, without going through the gate approval flow. A model can: (1) call forge_update_run with status:'completed' on a run that is in 'gate-pending', bypassing the gate entirely, or (2) reset a 'gate-pending' run back to 'running' to avoid the gate checkpoint and continue the pipeline. The mode-hardening check (validateModeForRisk) is only applied at forge_create_run and forge_resume_run — forge_update_run applies no such validation. The gate self-approval guard (hasGateApprovalToken) only protects forge_set_gate, not forge_update_run's status field.
findings.exploitability: High — the model can simply call forge_update_run({ runId: 'r-xxx', status: 'completed' }) to skip any pending gate. No preconditions required beyond knowing the runId, which the model always has.

findings.title: Gate approval token is not consumed after use — replayable within TTL
findings.description: hasGateApprovalToken (server.js line 73) reads .pipeline/action-approved.json, checks expiry, and returns true — but never deletes or invalidates the token. The token has a 120-second TTL (approval-token.js line 22). Within that window, the model can call forge_set_gate with status:'approved' an unlimited number of times, approving multiple gates from a single user 'approve' utterance. Scenario: user says 'approve' once (intending to approve gate1). Token is written. Model calls forge_set_gate to approve gate1, then immediately creates a new implement run, races through to gate2, and approves gate2 with the same still-valid token — all without the user seeing or approving the gate2 content.
findings.exploitability: High — the token exists for 120 seconds. The model controls timing and can chain multiple gate approvals within a single turn. The user has no visibility into subsequent approvals.

findings.title: forge_update_config allows pipelineMode downgrade to bypass reviewers
findings.description: forge_update_config (server.js line 415) allows setting pipelineMode to any string value — there is no enum validation against the known modes (TRIVIAL/SPRINT/LEAN/STANDARD/FULL). The type check (line 435) only verifies typeof === 'string'. A model can call forge_update_config({ key: 'pipelineMode', value: 'TRIVIAL' }) to downgrade the project's default pipeline mode. While forge_create_run has validateModeForRisk, TRIVIAL is explicitly allowed for 'plan' and 'apply' pipelines (line 100). More critically, SPRINT is allowed for implement/debug/refactor when the feature description doesn't match RISK_KEYWORDS — and the model controls the feature string. By sanitizing risk keywords from the feature description before passing it, the model bypasses the SPRINT block and runs without reviewers.
findings.exploitability: High — the model directly controls both the pipelineMode config value and the feature string passed to forge_create_run. It can craft a feature description that avoids RISK_KEYWORDS while still performing risky work.

findings.title: forge_update_agent_model can redirect reviewers to non-Anthropic providers
findings.description: forge_update_agent_model (server.js line 1004) allows changing allowedVendors for any agent in agentModelMap. A model can call forge_update_agent_model({ agentName: 'reviewer-safety', allowedVendors: ['openai'] }) to redirect the safety reviewer to OpenAI (or Gemini free tier), contradicting the project policy that all reviewers must route to Anthropic. The tool validates that agentName exists in agentModelMap but does not enforce any vendor constraints. This can be used to route reviewers to weaker/cheaper models that produce less thorough reviews, or to a provider the attacker controls.
findings.exploitability: Medium — requires the model to make a deliberate tool call. The routing change persists in forge-config.json and affects all subsequent pipeline runs until manually corrected. The user would not notice unless they inspect the config.

findings.title: forge_reset_usage allows unlimited quota reset to circumvent exhaustion guards
findings.description: forge_reset_usage (server.js line 938) resets all quotaExhausted flags with no authorization check. If a provider's quota is genuinely exhausted (e.g. billing limit hit), the model can call forge_reset_usage to clear the flag and immediately retry, potentially running up charges or hitting rate limits repeatedly. Combined with forge_call_external's automatic rerouting, this creates a loop: call fails with quota error -> markModelQuotaExhausted fires -> model calls forge_reset_usage -> retries indefinitely. There is no rate limit on reset calls.
findings.exploitability: Medium — the model must deliberately call forge_reset_usage. The financial impact depends on whether the external provider enforces hard limits beyond what FORGE tracks. Realistic in a scenario where the model is trying to complete a task and encounters quota blocks.

findings.title: forge_call_external prompt content is not bounded or validated
findings.description: forge_call_external (server.js line 791) forwards the `prompt` parameter directly to external APIs (OpenAI, Gemini) with no size limit, content filtering, or origin verification. A manipulated model can exfiltrate project data by encoding file contents into the prompt string and sending it to an external API endpoint. The response text is then returned to the model. While the model already has file read access, this creates a data exfiltration channel that bypasses any local-only controls — the data leaves the machine to a third-party API. The maxTokens parameter defaults to 4096 but the input prompt has no cap.
findings.exploitability: Medium — requires the model to be manipulated via prompt injection. The external call is visible in tool use logs, but the prompt content itself may not be audited. Data flows to legitimate API endpoints (api.openai.com, googleapis.com), making network-level detection difficult.
